h1Title: Adenovirus Infection: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Adenovirus infection is caused by a non-enveloped double-stranded DNA virus of the Adenoviridae family, comprising more than 90 genetically distinct human types grouped into species A-G. Adenoviruses collectively cause an estimated 5-10% of all acute febrile illnesses in children and 1-7% of community-acquired pneumonias across all age groups. The same virus can cause pharyngitis, conjunctivitis, pneumonia, gastroenteritis, hemorrhagic cystitis, or encephalitis depending on the serotype and the host's immune status. Most infections in immunocompetent individuals are self-limited within 7-14 days. In immunocompromised patients — particularly bone marrow transplant recipients — adenovirus causes multiorgan disseminated disease with mortality reaching 60-80%. No antiviral therapy has received regulatory approval; cidofovir is used off-label in severe or disseminated cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Adenoviruses account for 5-10% of acute respiratory illness in children and 1-7% of community-acquired pneumonia overall; seroprevalence approaches 100% in adults by age 5-10 years
keyStats.demographics: All ages affected; most severe disease in children under 5, military recruits (oral vaccine available for types 4 and 7), and immunocompromised patients; no sex predominance
keyStats.avgAge: Primary infections predominantly in children under 5; severe pneumonia clusters occur in military recruits aged 18-30 and immunosuppressed adults
keyStats.globalCases: Global ubiquity; epidemics of types 4, 7, and 14 documented in military recruit training camps; type 41 is a leading cause of viral gastroenteritis in children worldwide

definition: Adenovirus infection (ICD-10: B34.0) encompasses a spectrum of illnesses caused by the 90+ serotypes of Adenoviridae, classified into human adenoviruses (HAdV) species A through G. The virus is non-enveloped and resistant to lipid solvents, detergents, and drying — properties that facilitate environmental persistence on surfaces for days to weeks and transmission via fomites, respiratory droplets, ocular secretions, fecal-oral routes, and contaminated water. The virus enters target cells through binding of the fiber protein to CAR (coxsackie-adenovirus receptor) and integrin co-receptors; after internalization, dsDNA enters the nucleus and hijacks host transcription. Cell tropism determines clinical syndrome: types 1-7 cause respiratory tract disease; type 8, 19, and 37 cause epidemic keratoconjunctivitis; types 40-41 cause gastroenteritis in children; and types 11, 21, and 34 cause hemorrhagic cystitis. In immunocompetent hosts, innate and T-cell responses resolve infection without antiviral therapy. In hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT) recipients, CMV- or steroid-driven T-cell depletion enables adenovirus to replicate unchecked in the bowel and spread hematogenously to liver, kidney, lung, and brain. Pediatricians, internists, and infectious disease specialists manage the broad spectrum of adenoviral disease.

typesClassification.description: The most common presentation: pharyngitis, tonsillitis, laryngitis, or pharyngoconjunctival fever (types 3 and 7) with fever, exudative tonsils, and bilateral conjunctivitis. Clinically indistinguishable from streptococcal pharyngitis without rapid testing.

Caused primarily by types 3, 4, 7, 14, and 21. Ranges from atypical pneumonia indistinguishable from Mycoplasma to severe bilateral consolidation requiring mechanical ventilation. Type 7 and 14 have caused military recruit outbreaks with 20-40% hospitalization rates.

Caused by types 8, 19, and 37. Highly contagious waterborne or contact-spread infection causing unilateral or bilateral follicular conjunctivitis with corneal punctate keratitis, photophobia, and subepithelial corneal opacities that can impair vision for months.
Types 40 and 41 (enteric adenoviruses) cause watery diarrhea for 5-12 days in children under 5, accounting for 5-20% of viral gastroenteritis requiring hospitalization in this age group.
Types 11 and 21 cause gross hematuria, dysuria, and frequency in children and immunocompromised adults, particularly after bone marrow transplantation. Hematuria can be severe and prolonged.

Occurs almost exclusively in HSCT and solid-organ transplant recipients, and in patients with primary immunodeficiencies. Multiorgan disease with hepatitis, pneumonitis, nephritis, colitis, and encephalitis carries mortality of 60-80% without treatment.

primarySymptoms: Fever of 38-40°C lasting 3-7 days, often higher and more prolonged than other viral respiratory infections — present in virtually all symptomatic cases.

Exudative pharyngitis with tonsillar enlargement and white or grey exudate, clinically indistinguishable from Group A streptococcal pharyngitis and accounting for many unnecessary antibiotic prescriptions.
Acute follicular conjunctivitis with conjunctival redness, discharge, and lid edema — bilateral in many cases, unilateral or sequential in epidemic keratoconjunctivitis caused by types 8 and 19.
Dry or productive cough with crackles on auscultation in adenoviral pneumonia, which may progress over 3-7 days from mild upper respiratory symptoms to lobar or multilobar infiltrates.
Watery diarrhea lasting 5-12 days without blood or mucus in children with enteric adenovirus (types 40 and 41) gastroenteritis — a leading cause of post-rotavirus vaccine gastroenteritis.
Painful gross hematuria with frequency and dysuria from hemorrhagic cystitis in immunocompromised patients, particularly HSCT recipients in the first 100 days post-transplant.
Cervical lymphadenopathy, often tender, in the anterior cervical chain during pharyngeal or tonsillar adenoviral disease — can be significant enough to prompt lymphoma workup.
Photophobia and eye pain from corneal subepithelial infiltrates in epidemic keratoconjunctivitis — these infiltrates can persist for months and transiently reduce visual acuity.
Headache, myalgia, and malaise during the febrile phase of respiratory adenovirus infection, resembling influenza but usually without the severe prostration typical of influenza A.
Jaundice, hepatomegaly, and elevated transaminases in disseminated disease — adenoviral hepatitis in HSCT recipients can cause acute liver failure.
earlyWarningSigns: Fever with conjunctivitis and pharyngitis simultaneously — the pharyngoconjunctival fever triad is highly characteristic of adenoviral infection and should prompt virologic testing rather than empiric antibiotics
Bilateral conjunctivitis with follicular reaction appearing in multiple members of a household or childcare cohort — adenovirus epidemic keratoconjunctivitis spreads rapidly via hands and fomites
Rising adenovirus DNA viral load on PCR surveillance testing in an HSCT recipient within the first 60 days post-transplant — asymptomatic viremia predicts imminent organ dissemination
Gross hematuria appearing within the first 100 days after bone marrow transplantation — hemorrhagic cystitis from adenovirus requires urgent virologic confirmation
emergencySigns: Severe hypoxia (O2 saturation <92%) with bilateral infiltrates in an immunocompromised patient — adenoviral pneumonitis progressing to ARDS carries 60-80% mortality without cidofovir initiation
Jaundice, coagulopathy, and encephalopathy in an HSCT recipient — adenoviral acute liver failure requires urgent antiviral treatment and transplant team involvement
New seizures or altered mental status in an immunocompromised patient with systemic adenoviral viremia — CNS adenovirus encephalitis requiring MRI brain, lumbar puncture, and antiviral therapy
Severe hemorrhagic cystitis with clot retention causing urinary obstruction — requires urgent urology consultation and potential cystoscopy with clot evacuation

causes.description: Adenovirus is shed in nasopharyngeal secretions and spreads via respiratory droplets and small aerosols during coughing, sneezing, or talking. Closed environments — military barracks, daycare centers, hospitals, and schools — facilitate rapid transmission. Respiratory types (1-7) remain viable in droplets for hours and on surfaces for days.

Enteric adenoviruses (types 40, 41) are shed in high concentrations in stool for weeks after infection. Transmission occurs via contaminated water, unwashed hands, food, and surfaces in childcare settings. Chlorination eliminates most enteric adenoviruses from treated water; swimming pools inadequately chlorinated have caused outbreaks.

causes.cause: Direct contact and fomite transmission (ocular types)
causes.description: Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis (types 8, 19, 37) spreads via contaminated fingers, eye examination equipment, and ophthalmology instruments. Healthcare worker-to-patient transmission during eye exams without proper disinfection has caused institutional outbreaks. The virus survives on tonometer tips and slit-lamp equipment for 30 days.

Inadequately chlorinated swimming pools, lakes, and recreational water sources transmit pharyngoconjunctival fever (types 3 and 7) via contaminated water entering the conjunctivae and oropharynx. The virus resists standard disinfection levels and requires higher chlorine concentrations (>2 ppm) for elimination.

Adenovirus establishes latency in tonsillar lymphoid tissue and the gut mucosa in healthy individuals after primary infection. T-cell depletion after HSCT (particularly following ATG or alemtuzumab conditioning) or following immunosuppression for GvHD reactivates latent adenovirus from the gut into the bloodstream, with rapid systemic spread.

riskFactors.description: Children have not yet acquired serotype-specific immunity and are susceptible to sequential infections with different adenovirus types. Children under 5 account for the majority of adenoviral gastroenteritis hospitalizations and are more likely to develop pneumonia from respiratory types.

HSCT recipients, particularly those receiving T-cell-depleted grafts or antithymocyte globulin, are at highest risk for adenoviral dissemination and death. Adenoviral disease occurs in 3-18% of pediatric HSCT recipients and 5-10% of adult HSCT recipients in contemporary series, with mortality of 20-80%.

Children with T-cell defects, including SCID, DiGeorge syndrome, and Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome, are at extreme risk for fatal disseminated adenoviral disease even from community-acquired strains that are harmless in immunocompetent hosts.

Close-quarters living, physical stress, and mixing of recruits from different geographic origins create ideal conditions for adenovirus types 4 and 7 outbreaks. Before oral adenovirus vaccine introduction in the 1970s (and after its withdrawal in 1999-2011), attack rates in training camps reached 70-80%.

Childcare centers and daycare facilities are the primary amplifying sites for respiratory and enteric adenoviruses in young children. Outbreaks in a single facility can affect 30-70% of enrolled children within 2-4 weeks.

Kidney, liver, and heart transplant recipients can develop primary adenoviral infection or reactivation, particularly when receiving high-dose immunosuppression for rejection episodes. Adenoviral hepatitis and nephritis have caused allograft loss.

affectedDemographics: Children under 5: the primary age group for severe respiratory and enteric adenoviral disease; adenoviruses are the leading or second leading cause of viral respiratory disease in this age group globally.

Military recruits: types 4 and 7 have caused explosive outbreaks in US Army and Navy recruit training, with overall attack rates above 60% before oral adenovirus type 4/7 vaccine reintroduction in 2011.

HSCT recipients: 3-18% of pediatric HSCT patients develop adenoviral disease in the early post-transplant period; the incidence is highest with cord blood or mismatched donor transplants using T-cell depletion.

Immunocompromised children: adenovirus is the third most common cause of life-threatening respiratory viral infection in pediatric cancer patients after RSV and parainfluenza, per Children's Oncology Group data.
Elderly patients in long-term care: institutional respiratory adenovirus outbreaks can affect 20-40% of residents with significant morbidity in frail elderly populations.
diagnosisOverview:
Diagnosis of adenovirus infection in immunocompetent patients is primarily clinical — pharyngoconjunctival fever, epidemic keratoconjunctivitis, or acute respiratory illness with the right epidemiologic context (childcare exposure, military barracks, swimming pool outbreak) is sufficient for presumptive diagnosis without laboratory confirmation in routine cases. Rapid molecular testing has changed management in two key settings: identifying adenovirus as the cause of severe pneumonia (where PCR result changes antibiotic decisions) and monitoring HSCT recipients for early viremia before dissemination occurs.

Nasopharyngeal swab or BAL PCR for adenovirus is the most sensitive diagnostic method, identifying serotype and quantifying viral load — both relevant for treatment decisions. Sensitivity of PCR exceeds 95% for active respiratory infection. For enteric disease, stool PCR is preferred over antigen testing due to higher sensitivity for types 40 and 41. Rapid lateral flow antigen tests for adenovirus are available for point-of-care use (sensitivity 50-80% for respiratory types), adequate for outbreak management in schools or military settings. Adenovirus can be cultured from respiratory, stool, urine, or tissue samples, but culture adds little over PCR in clinical management. Serology (neutralizing antibody) is used for outbreak investigations but is of limited clinical utility for individual diagnosis given universal prior exposure in adults.

In immunocompromised patients, whole-blood or plasma adenovirus quantitative PCR is monitored weekly during the early post-transplant period; viral loads above 1,000 copies/mL are associated with progression to disseminated disease and trigger pre-emptive antiviral therapy in many centers.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Most sensitive test for respiratory adenoviral disease; identifies serotype and quantifies viral load
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab inserted briefly into the nostril or back of the throat — takes under 30 seconds. Results within 2-6 hours on modern molecular platforms. Serotype data available within 24-48 hours from most reference labs.

diagnosticTests.test: Quantitative whole-blood or plasma adenovirus PCR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Monitors viral load in immunocompromised patients for early detection of viremia and pre-emptive treatment; guides antiviral dosing and treatment duration
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. Quantitative results in 24-48 hours. Viral load above 1,000 copies/mL signals need for antiviral consideration in HSCT recipients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses enteric adenoviral gastroenteritis (types 40-41) with high sensitivity; distinguishes from bacterial causes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fresh stool sample collected in a clean container. Results in 1-3 days. Preferred over antigen testing for sensitivity and serotype identification. Positive result supports symptomatic management without antibiotics.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Point-of-care diagnosis in acute respiratory illness — useful for outbreak management and rapid clinical decision-making
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Nasal or throat swab results available in 15-30 minutes. Sensitivity 50-80% for respiratory types; a negative result does not exclude adenovirus in moderate or high clinical suspicion. Adequate for outbreak screening.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes pulmonary infiltrates, bilateral consolidation, or interstitial patterns in adenoviral pneumonia; guides severity assessment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 15-minute scan without IV contrast for initial evaluation. Bilateral patchy ground-glass opacities or consolidation are common in moderate-to-severe adenoviral pneumonia. Report within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnoses adenoviral hemorrhagic cystitis in immunocompromised patients and excludes bacterial cystitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard urine sample. Quantitative PCR result in 24-48 hours. High viral loads in urine correlate with symptom severity in hemorrhagic cystitis.

treatmentOverview:
There is no FDA-approved antiviral agent specifically for adenovirus infection. Treatment of adenoviral disease in immunocompetent individuals is entirely supportive: rest, hydration, fever reduction with acetaminophen or ibuprofen, and symptomatic management of pharyngitis, conjunctivitis, or diarrhea. Antibiotics should not be prescribed for adenoviral infection without evidence of bacterial superinfection — adenovirus pharyngitis is one of the most common drivers of unnecessary antibiotic prescriptions in outpatient medicine.

In immunocompromised patients, particularly HSCT recipients with significant adenoviral viremia or disseminated disease, cidofovir is used off-label at 1-5 mg/kg IV weekly with probenecid and IV hydration to reduce nephrotoxicity. Cidofovir inhibits adenoviral DNA polymerase but has limited clinical data: controlled trials are lacking, and retrospective series report viral load reduction in 50-70% of patients with variable clinical benefit. Brincidofovir (hexadecyloxypropyl-cidofovir; CMX001), an oral lipid-conjugated cidofovir prodrug with more favorable pharmacokinetics and higher intracellular concentrations, showed activity against adenovirus in phase 2 trials and is used compassionately in several countries. A key principle in HSCT-related adenoviral disease is reduction of immunosuppression wherever clinically feasible — T-cell reconstitution is ultimately the most effective treatment, as adenoviral clearance tracks closely with recovery of adenovirus-specific T-cell immunity.

Adenovirus-specific T-cell therapy using donor-derived or third-party adenovirus-specific T lymphocytes is emerging from clinical trials with response rates of 60-75% in refractory disease, representing a promising therapeutic approach for the most severely immunocompromised patients who cannot reconstitute T-cell immunity by other means.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive care (fluids, antipyretics, rest)
medicalTreatments.description: Mainstay of treatment for all immunocompetent adenoviral infections. Oral rehydration for gastroenteritis, acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever and pharyngeal pain, and artificial tears for conjunctivitis. No antiviral drug is needed or proven beneficial for routine cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Self-limited resolution in 7-14 days in over 95% of immunocompetent patients without antiviral therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Cidofovir (1-5 mg/kg IV weekly with probenecid co-administration)
medicalTreatments.description: Off-label antiviral used in severe adenoviral disease in immunocompromised patients. Inhibits viral DNA polymerase. Nephrotoxicity is the major dose-limiting toxicity; probenecid and IV hydration are required with each dose. Dose adjustment for baseline renal impairment is essential.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Viral load reduction in 50-70% of treated immunocompromised patients in retrospective series; survival benefit not established in controlled trials

medicalTreatments.name: Brincidofovir (oral prodrug of cidofovir, 100 mg twice weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral lipid-conjugated cidofovir with greater intracellular concentration and less nephrotoxicity than parenteral cidofovir. Available on compassionate use and as clinical trial protocol in many centers. Shown to reduce adenoviral viremia in immunocompromised patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Phase 2 data show viremia reduction in 60-70% of HSCT patients with adenoviral disease; diarrhea is the primary dose-limiting side-effect

medicalTreatments.name: Reduction of immunosuppression (HSCT patients)
medicalTreatments.description: Tapering steroids, reducing calcineurin inhibitors, or withdrawing mycophenolate accelerates T-cell reconstitution, which is the primary mechanism of viral clearance. Managed carefully against the risk of triggering graft-versus-host disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adenoviral clearance closely follows T-cell reconstitution; viral load declines in proportion to CD3 T-cell recovery; the most durable intervention

medicalTreatments.name: Adenovirus-specific T-cell therapy (donor-derived or third-party)
medicalTreatments.description: Adoptive transfer of adenovirus-specific T lymphocytes expanded ex vivo from transplant donors or matched third-party donors. Used as salvage therapy in cidofovir-refractory disseminated disease. Clinical response rates of 60-75% in published series.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates 60-75% in compassionate use series (Feuchtinger 2010, Leen 2013); duration of response variable; potentially curative in patients who achieve durable T-cell reconstitution

surgicalOptions.name: Cystoscopy with clot evacuation for hemorrhagic cystitis
surgicalOptions.description: Severe adenoviral hemorrhagic cystitis with clot retention and urinary obstruction requires cystoscopic clot evacuation and continuous bladder irrigation. Performed by urology under general or spinal anesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom relief in 70-85% of cases after clot clearance; recurrence common while viral shedding continues without antiviral control

whySeeSpecialist: Most adenoviral infections require no specialist care. Infectious disease consultation is appropriate for immunocompromised patients with significant viremia or disseminated disease, severe adenoviral pneumonia requiring antiviral therapy, and HSCT recipients where pre-emptive treatment decisions need expertise in transplant infectious disease.
doctorSelectionGuide: For HSCT-related adenoviral disease, seek an infectious disease specialist affiliated with a bone marrow transplant program. For severe pneumonia in immunocompetent patients not resolving with supportive care, a pulmonologist can guide bronchoscopy and BAL diagnosis. Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis is best managed by an experienced ophthalmologist.
hospitalCriteria: Hypoxia or respiratory failure from adenoviral pneumonia requiring supplemental oxygen or mechanical ventilation
HSCT recipient with adenoviral viremia above 1,000 copies/mL or evidence of disseminated organ involvement
Immunocompromised child with high fever and any combination of respiratory, GI, or hepatic adenoviral involvement
Severe hemorrhagic cystitis with urinary retention or bilateral obstructive uropathy
Suspected adenoviral encephalitis or meningitis requiring IV access for hydration and imaging
keyFacilities: Bone marrow transplant centers with transplant infectious disease specialists
Pediatric infectious disease units with experience in immunocompromised viral infections
Ophthalmology clinics for epidemic keratoconjunctivitis management and corneal opacity follow-up
Pediatric intensive care units for severe adenoviral pneumonia in young children
preventionStrategies: Practice rigorous hand hygiene — wash hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds after touching eyes, nose, or mouth, and after contact with sick individuals.
Maintain adequate chlorine levels (above 2 ppm) in swimming pools and hot tubs to inactivate adenoviruses responsible for pharyngoconjunctival fever outbreaks.

Ensure all ophthalmological equipment (tonometer tips, slit-lamp eyepieces) is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite or povidone-iodine between patients to prevent EKC transmission in clinical settings.
Military recruits should receive the oral adenovirus types 4 and 7 live vaccine, which has reduced outbreak attack rates in recruit training camps by over 95% since its reintroduction in 2011.
Weekly adenovirus surveillance PCR monitoring in high-risk HSCT recipients during the early post-transplant period enables pre-emptive antiviral intervention before dissemination.
lifestyleModifications: Stay home from school, childcare, or work while febrile or with active conjunctival discharge — most adenoviral infections are contagious for 3-5 days after symptom onset.
Avoid sharing towels, pillowcases, or eye cosmetics during active conjunctivitis — adenoviruses are highly stable on surfaces and indirect transmission is common.
Maintain hand hygiene before and after administering any eye drops or wiping eye discharge to prevent spread to the other eye and household contacts.

Complete all advised follow-up appointments for corneal adenoviral infiltrates — subepithelial infiltrates after epidemic keratoconjunctivitis can persist for months and require topical steroid management under ophthalmologic care.

For HSCT recipients: report any gross hematuria, diarrhea, new respiratory symptoms, or fever to the transplant team immediately as these may indicate adenoviral dissemination requiring urgent viral load testing.
Avoid daycare or group childcare for affected children until diarrhea resolves completely and fever is absent for 24 hours.

recommended: Oral rehydration solution (ORS) with appropriate sodium and glucose balance for adenoviral gastroenteritis in children — preferred over plain water or sports drinks
BRAT diet (bananas, rice, applesauce, toast) or other bland low-fiber foods during the acute diarrheal phase
Cool, soft foods and cold liquids to soothe adenoviral pharyngitis and tonsillar inflammation
Adequate caloric intake during recovery — illness-associated anorexia in children can lead to significant weight loss during prolonged enteric adenovirus infection
avoid: Dairy products during active adenoviral gastroenteritis if transient lactase deficiency develops
High-sugar beverages (sodas, undiluted juices) which can worsen osmotic diarrhea during acute gastroenteritis
Caffeine and alcohol in adults during adenoviral illness as they worsen dehydration
Hard or scratchy foods that exacerbate pharyngeal pain in adenoviral pharyngitis

exerciseGuidelines: Complete rest during the febrile phase (typically 3-7 days). Light activity can resume once fever resolves and energy returns. Contact sports should be avoided while conjunctivitis is active due to fomite transmission risk. Immunocompromised patients should follow transplant team guidance before resuming any exercise during antiviral treatment.
dailyManagement: Take temperature twice daily during the acute febrile phase and use acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever above 38.5°C.
Monitor hydration status: ensure urine output every 4-6 hours in children with gastroenteritis; use ORS for replacement in mild-moderate dehydration.
Apply preservative-free artificial tears every 2-4 hours for conjunctivitis to reduce irritation; avoid contact lens wear until symptoms fully resolve.
HSCT recipients: check adenovirus PCR blood results at each monitoring visit; report any new symptoms — hematuria, diarrhea, cough — immediately.
Avoid sharing personal items (towels, glasses, utensils) during active illness to prevent household transmission.
Track symptom progression — worsening dyspnea, new confusion, increasing jaundice, or blood in urine should prompt same-day physician contact.
prognosis: Prognosis for adenoviral infection in immunocompetent individuals is excellent: more than 95% of cases resolve fully within 7-14 days without specific therapy or lasting organ damage. Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis may leave subepithelial corneal infiltrates causing photophobia and reduced visual acuity for up to 1-2 years, though most cases resolve completely. The prognosis in immunocompromised patients is dramatically worse: HSCT recipients with disseminated adenoviral disease have historical mortality rates of 60-80%. Modern antiviral approaches (cidofovir, brincidofovir) combined with immunosuppression reduction improve outcomes but mortality remains 20-40% in patients with multiorgan disease. Early detection through weekly viral load monitoring and pre-emptive treatment before dissemination — now standard practice at most HSCT centers — has reduced dissemination rates from 15-18% to 8-12% in contemporary cohorts. Adenoviral-specific T-cell therapy offers the best outcomes in refractory disease when immunosuppression cannot be safely reduced.
recoveryTimeline: Fever resolves in 3-7 days in immunocompetent individuals. Pharyngitis and conjunctivitis improve over 7-10 days. Gastroenteritis resolves in 5-12 days. Corneal subepithelial infiltrates from EKC may persist 1-24 months. In HSCT patients, viral load typically becomes undetectable within 4-8 weeks of combined antiviral and immunosuppression reduction when T-cell reconstitution occurs.
complications: Adenoviral pneumonia with ARDS: occurs in immunocompromised patients and in severe exposures to virulent serotypes (types 7 and 14); requires mechanical ventilation and carries mortality of 20-40%.

Subepithelial corneal infiltrates from epidemic keratoconjunctivitis: can reduce visual acuity and cause photophobia for months; recurrence triggered by UV light and stress, managed with topical steroids under ophthalmologic care.

Disseminated adenoviral disease in HSCT recipients: multiorgan failure involving liver, lung, kidney, gut, and CNS; mortality 60-80% without antiviral therapy; managed with cidofovir, immunosuppression reduction, and T-cell therapy.
Hemorrhagic cystitis: severe hematuria with clot retention in HSCT patients; can progress to bilateral ureteric obstruction and renal failure.
Intussusception: rare complication of adenoviral mesenteric adenitis in children under 2 years — abdominal pain with 'currant jelly' stools requires urgent ultrasound and air-enema reduction.
Post-infectious bronchiolitis obliterans: rare sequela of severe adenoviral type 7 pneumonia in children, causing chronic obliterative airway disease resembling asthma that may persist for years.

supportResources.name: CDC Adenovirus Information
supportResources.description: CDC patient-facing and clinical information on adenovirus types, spread, symptoms, and prevention.

supportResources.name: Infectious Diseases Society of America — Adenovirus Resources
supportResources.description: Clinical guidelines and professional resources for adenoviral disease management, including transplant infectious disease guidance.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Epidemic Keratoconjunctivitis
supportResources.description: Patient education on EKC symptoms, transmission prevention, and corneal infiltrate management from the US ophthalmology professional body.

confusedWithConditions.name: Adenoviral pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Adenoviral pneumonia is a specific lower respiratory complication of adenovirus infection coded separately; unspecified adenoviral infection (B34.0) covers the broader spectrum. Pneumonia requires chest imaging confirmation and typically antiviral consideration in high-risk patients; uncomplicated adenoviral URTI does not.

confusedWithConditions.name: Infectious Mononucleosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause exudative pharyngitis with fever and cervical lymphadenopathy, but mononucleosis (EBV) produces a positive monospot or EBV-specific antibodies, splenomegaly, and characteristic atypical lymphocytosis on CBC. Adenovirus rarely causes splenomegaly; monospot testing and EBV serology distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Upper Respiratory Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Adenovirus is one of many causes of URI alongside rhinovirus, coronavirus, and influenza. The combination of fever with conjunctivitis (pharyngoconjunctival fever) is characteristic of adenovirus and distinguishes it from rhinoviral common cold, which causes minimal fever and no conjunctivitis. Rapid adenovirus PCR provides confirmation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Parvovirus Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Parvovirus B19 causes erythema infectiosum (fifth disease) with facial 'slapped cheek' rash and aplastic crisis in hemolytic anemia patients — manifestations not seen with adenovirus. Both can cause fever in children, but serological and PCR tests readily differentiate them.

relatedConditions.name: Adenoviral pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Lower respiratory complication of adenovirus infection; requires specific diagnostic and treatment pathways distinct from uncomplicated adenoviral URI.

relatedConditions.name: Adenoviral encephalitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Rare CNS manifestation of adenovirus dissemination, primarily in immunocompromised patients; linked to the same virological etiology.

relatedConditions.name: Coronavirus infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Another respiratory RNA virus causing overlapping clinical syndrome with adenovirus; PCR multiplexing commonly diagnoses both alongside adenovirus in respiratory panels.

relatedConditions.name: Parvovirus infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Common childhood viral infection sharing similar epidemiological setting in daycare and school environments; both cause febrile illness with variable rash and respiratory components.

faqs.question: What is adenovirus infection?
faqs.answer: Adenovirus infection is a viral illness caused by any of more than 90 human adenovirus types that can infect the respiratory tract, eyes, gut, urinary tract, or — in immunocompromised patients — multiple organs simultaneously. Most infections in healthy people are self-limited and resolve without antiviral treatment.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of adenovirus?
faqs.answer: Symptoms depend on the type but commonly include fever, sore throat with tonsil exudate, runny nose, conjunctivitis, and cough lasting 7-14 days. Some types cause watery diarrhea, especially in children under 5. Severe infection can cause pneumonia. The combination of fever, sore throat, and red eyes together is characteristic of adenovirus.

faqs.question: How do you get adenovirus?
faqs.answer: Adenovirus spreads by respiratory droplets from coughing or sneezing, direct contact with infected surfaces (fomites), contaminated water in swimming pools, and fecal-oral routes in childcare settings. Shared towels, bedding, or eye drops from an infected person also transmit the virus. It is highly contagious in households and daycare settings.

faqs.question: How long does adenovirus last?
faqs.answer: In healthy individuals, adenoviral illness typically lasts 7-14 days. Fever resolves in 3-7 days. Conjunctivitis improves over 7-10 days but corneal infiltrates from epidemic keratoconjunctivitis can persist 1-2 years. Adenoviral gastroenteritis in children resolves in 5-12 days.

faqs.question: Is there an antiviral treatment for adenovirus?
faqs.answer: There is no FDA-approved antiviral specifically for adenovirus. Immunocompetent patients require only supportive care. In severe disease in immunocompromised patients, cidofovir (IV) or brincidofovir (oral) are used off-label. Antibiotics are not effective against adenovirus and should be avoided without evidence of bacterial superinfection.

faqs.question: Is adenovirus contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes, adenovirus is highly contagious. It spreads via respiratory droplets, direct contact, contaminated surfaces, and fecal-oral routes. Infected people are typically contagious for 3-5 days after symptom onset, though the virus can be shed in stool for weeks. It can survive on surfaces for days, making hand hygiene critical.

faqs.question: Can adenovirus cause pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adenovirus types 3, 4, 7, 14, and 21 can cause pneumonia ranging from mild atypical pneumonia to severe bilateral consolidation requiring mechanical ventilation. Type 7 and 14 caused military recruit outbreaks with hospitalization rates of 20-40%. Severe adenoviral pneumonia in immunocompromised patients carries mortality of 60-80%.

faqs.question: How is adenovirus diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Most adenoviral infections are diagnosed clinically. When confirmation is needed, nasopharyngeal PCR is the most sensitive test and identifies the specific serotype. Rapid antigen tests are available for point-of-care use. For immunocompromised patients, quantitative blood PCR monitors viral load and guides antiviral treatment decisions.

faqs.question: Can adults get adenovirus?
faqs.answer: Yes. Adults can develop adenoviral illness, particularly when encountering a new serotype they lack immunity to, during exposure to crowded environments like military barracks, or when immunocompromised. Most adults have immunity to common types from childhood infections, but over 90 human serotypes mean re-infection with unfamiliar types is possible.

faqs.question: Is adenovirus the same as conjunctivitis (pink eye)?
faqs.answer: Adenovirus is the most common cause of infectious conjunctivitis (pink eye), responsible for 65-90% of viral conjunctivitis cases. Epidemic keratoconjunctivitis, caused by types 8, 19, and 37, is a particularly contagious severe form that spreads in ophthalmology clinics, schools, and sports facilities and can cause corneal scarring.

faqs.question: Why is adenovirus dangerous in transplant patients?
faqs.answer: Bone marrow transplant recipients lack T-cell immunity after their transplant conditioning. Without T cells to control viral replication, adenovirus can spread from the gut or lungs into the bloodstream and damage the liver, kidneys, lungs, and brain simultaneously. Disseminated adenoviral disease carries mortality of 60-80% in this population.

faqs.question: Can you get adenovirus from a swimming pool?
faqs.answer: Yes. Inadequately chlorinated swimming pools are a documented source of pharyngoconjunctival fever, caused by adenovirus types 3 and 7. The virus enters through the eyes and throat during swimming. Maintaining pool chlorine levels above 2 ppm eliminates most adenoviral transmission. Ocean and lake swimming carry similar risk.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for adenovirus?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only for the US military. An oral live-attenuated vaccine against adenovirus types 4 and 7 is available to military recruits aged 17-50 and has virtually eliminated type 4/7 outbreaks at training camps since its reintroduction in 2011. No adenovirus vaccine is licensed for civilian use.

faqs.question: How do you prevent spreading adenovirus at home?
faqs.answer: Wash hands frequently with soap and water for at least 20 seconds, especially after touching eyes, nose, or mouth. Do not share towels, pillowcases, or personal eye care items with an infected person. Disinfect frequently touched surfaces with a bleach-based cleaner. Keep infected children home from school until fever-free for 24 hours.

faqs.question: What is adenoviral hemorrhagic cystitis?
faqs.answer: Adenoviral hemorrhagic cystitis is a painful bladder inflammation causing gross hematuria, urinary urgency, and frequency, caused mainly by adenovirus types 11 and 21. It occurs primarily in bone marrow transplant recipients and immunocompromised children. Treatment is supportive; cidofovir is used in severe cases with urinary obstruction.

symptomKeywords: fever with red eyes and sore throat
pneumonia not responding to antibiotics viral
blood in urine after bone marrow transplant
